content.problemStatement:
Nasir and two of his henchmen are planning to attack NNN shops of the Qureshi clan. The shops are conveniently lined up, and numbered from 111 to NNN. The iii-th shop contains AiA_iAi​ kg of coal.

For a given subarray of shops [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \dots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​], the bitwise and of this subarray is defined to be the bitwise AND of the coal contained in each of the shops, i.e, AL&AL+1&…&ARA_L \& A_{L+1} \& \dots \& A_RAL​&AL+1​&…&AR​.

The damage caused when bombing the iii-th shop is computed to be the maximum bitwise and over all subarrays containing shop iii, and whose size is **strictly larger than 1**.

For each shop (1≤i≤N1\leq i\leq N1≤i≤N), find the damage caused when Nasir bombs it.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, denoting the number of shops.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, the iii-th of which is AiA_iAi​, representing the amount of coal in the iii-th shop.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN integers d1,d2,…,dnd_1,d_2,\ldots,d_nd1​,d2​,…,dn​ where did_idi​ represents the maximum damage that can be caused if the iii-th shop is to be bombed.
constraints: 2 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (number of shops). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the amount of coal in each shop.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N integers d1 … dN where di is the maximum possible bitwise AND of any subarray of length at least 2 that contains shop i.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: There are several choices of subarray which give the maximum for each index. One such choice is given below:
For shop 111, choose subarray [29,412,671,255][29, 412, 671, 255][29,412,671,255]
For shop 222, choose subarray [412,671,255][412, 671, 255][412,671,255]
For shop 333, choose subarray [671,255][671, 255][671,255]
For shop 444. choose subarray [671,255][671, 255][671,255]
For shop 555, choose subarray [412,671,255,912][412, 671, 255, 912][412,671,255,912]

solution.approach:
For any subarray, adding more elements can only clear bits, therefore the AND of a longer subarray is never larger than the AND of any of its length‑2 sub‑segments. Consequently, for a fixed position i the maximum AND among all qualifying subarrays is simply the maximum of the two possible length‑2 subarrays that contain i: (Ai‑1 & Ai) and (Ai & Ai+1) (if those neighbours exist). The answer for each i is:
    ans[i] = max( (i>1 ? A[i] & A[i-1] : 0), (i<N ? A[i] & A[i+1] : 0) ).
The algorithm scans the array once, computes the two neighbour ANDs and stores the maximum. Time complexity O(N) per test case, total O(sum N). Memory O(1) besides the input array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array of each test case (can be O(1) extra).
